variants.subject: {{property_address}}
note about the property
no repairs first
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

One reason I’m asking about {{property_address}} now is because the first step does not require the house to be retail-ready.

If selling as-is ever makes sense, it is usually because the owner would rather not deal with repairs, cleanout, showings, or a drawn-out listing process.

I can give you the simple version of what I look for, then you can decide if it is relevant.

Should I send that over?

— {{your_name}}

variants.subject: quick property question
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

One reason I’m asking about {{property_address}} now is because the first step does not require the house to be retail-ready.

If selling as-is ever makes sense, it is usually because the owner would rather not deal with repairs, cleanout, showings, or a drawn-out listing process.

variants.subject: family property question
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

Sometimes with a family property, the hard part is not just the house. It is deciding what to do, who is involved, whether repairs are worth it, and whether the normal listing route is worth the effort.

That is why I start with a practical as-is read instead of asking you to commit to anything.

Would you want to know whether {{property_address}} is even the kind of property I could look at?

— {{your_name}}

variants.subject: worth a quick look?

variants.subject: close this?
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

Last note from me about {{property_address}}.

Should I close this out, or would a quick as-is read be useful before the family spends time fixing, cleaning, listing, or gathering everything first?

Either way is fine.

— {{your_name}}
Reply “remove” and I’ll remove you.

instantly_payload.name: Email | Tax + Title Friction | Property Situation Review | Texas | 2026-05

variants.subject: {{property_address}}
note about the property
quick property question
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

Quick follow-up on {{property_address}}.

A normal sale can require repairs, cleanup, showings, paperwork, and waiting on the right retail buyer. That may be the best path for some owners.

But if convenience, privacy, timing, or avoiding more work matters more, I can give you a practical read on whether an as-is path is even realistic.

Want the simple version of what I look for?

— {{your_name}}

variants.subject: no repairs first
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

Quick follow-up on {{property_address}}.

A normal sale can require repairs, cleanup, showings, paperwork, and waiting on the right retail buyer. That may be the best path for some owners.

But if convenience, privacy, timing, or avoiding more work matters more, I can give you a practical read on whether an as-is path is even realistic.

Want the simple version of what I look for?

— {{your_name}}

variants.subject: as-is vs listing
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

For {{property_address}}, there are usually two very different paths.

A retail listing may bring a higher price if the property is ready, the paperwork is clean, and the owner wants to deal with showings and time on market.

A direct as-is option is usually for people who value simplicity, privacy, timing, or avoiding repairs and cleanup more than squeezing every dollar through a listing.

Would it be useful to know if the as-is path is even on the table?

— {{your_name}}

variants.subject: worth comparing?
variants.body:

variants.subject: should I close this?
variants.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

I do not want to keep following up if this is not relevant.

Should I close my notes on {{property_address}}, or would you want a quick read on whether an as-is option is worth discussing?

Either answer is fine.

— {{your_name}}
{{phone}}
